Title: 24-hour control centre activated after TTDI mud flood incident
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Emergency mitigation works and a round-the-clock control centre have been ordered following a mud flood that hit Jalan Haji Openg in Taman Tun Dr Ismail (TTDI) yesterday.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said several immediate measures have been activated to address the crisis.
The mitigation and rectification works include the construction of a third sedimentation and erosion and sediment control pond (ESCP), increasing the capacity of the existing second ESCP pond, and desilting and maintenance of the first pond.
Additional works involve maintenance of all unlined drains at the site, removal of collapsed structures, installation of New Jersey Barriers (NJB), traffic control coordination by the contractor together with the Public Works Department (PWD) and City Hall (DBKL), flushing of drainage systems, as well as ongoing cleaning and maintenance works.
"I have also requested immediate assistance and additional manpower from PWD headquarters, with PWD Selangor deploying extra machinery this morning to speed up repair works.
"A DBKL control centre has been set up and will operate around the clock for the duration of the crisis," she said in a Facebook post today.
An emergency meeting chaired by Kuala Lumpur mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud was held at 10am today, with reprentatives of all relevant agencies.
Yesterday, Yeoh said a
stop-work order
was issued with immediate effect on the Bukit Kiara Federal Park administrative zone office project following the mud flood, which resulted in the collapse of a retaining wall.
She also said DBKL issued a compound to the contractor, while cleaning works were carried out jointly with the Fire and Rescue Department and the contractor's team.
The project, under the Housing and Local Government Ministry's National Landscape Department, is being implemented by the PWD under the Works Ministry.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: GOF nabs smuggler for making repeated petrol trips for RM15 fee
Article:
TUMPAT: A smuggler who made repeated petrol purchases for a RM15 fee per trip was arrested by the General Operations Force (GOF) at the Pos Nibong shortcut yesterday morning.
The 9.50am arrest was the first made by the GOF since the agency was tasked with monitoring nine petrol stations along the Kelantan border.
Southeast Brigade GOF commander Senior Assistant Commissioner Ahmad Radzi Hussain said Battalion 9 personnel spotted a Mitsubishi Pajero making multiple petrol purchases within a single hour.
Suspecting foul play, officers trailed the four-wheel-drive vehicle before intercepting it at Pos Nibong.
"Upon inspection, we found the vehicle's tank full, and the 32-year-old suspect admitted to making repeated purchases at the station.
"He confessed that the first load had already been transferred into jerrycans at the Pok Su Roni illegal jetty before being ferried to Thailand by boat," Ahmad Radzi said when contacted.
The suspect revealed he had been involved in smuggling since last year after being recruited by his brother-in-law.
He reportedly made up to three trips a day, depending on demand from Thailand.
Investigations also found that the smugglers used the Zalo communication app to coordinate their activities and evade detection.
Ahmad Radzi said 90 litres of petrol and the vehicle, with a combined value of more than RM30,000, were seized.
The case is being investigated under Section 21 of the Control of Supplies Act 1961.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sim: Leaders must face public criticism, not hide
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: Leaders, including those in government, must not shy away from engaging on the ground and facing public criticism, even if it means being scolded by disgruntled citizens.
Penang DAP chief Steven Sim Chee Keong said it was a political reality that those in power would inevitably face dissatisfaction from the public over policy decisions, adding that such feedback should be accepted with humility.
"Today, not only in some countries but also at the federal level, when we become the government, we must listen to criticism from the people.
"There will be citizens who are unhappy with certain decisions. That is reality," he said.
Sim, who is also Entrepreneur and Cooperatives Development minister, recalled former DAP secretary-general Lim Guan Eng as having previously emphasised that leadership requires courage in facing public criticism.
"If you want to be a leader, if you want to be in government, you must be brave enough to face the people's criticism," he added.
Sim said that DAP itself had long encouraged public scrutiny of the government, including the right of citizens to criticise those in power.
"We are the ones who taught the people to monitor and criticise the government. So today, we must be brave and humble in accepting all comments and criticism from the people," he said.
Sim added that leaders should not only listen but also go down to the ground to explain government policies directly to the public, even in difficult environments such as markets where they may be confronted or scolded.
"I have heard some saying they do not dare go to the market because people will scold them.
"But if you do not go and explain the truth and the facts, others will fill that space with misinformation and slander," he pointed out.
He added that elected representatives had a responsibility to engage directly with communities to counter false narratives and explain government initiatives.
Sim also highlighted several policy and development efforts, including improvements in workers' welfare, education access, and infrastructure development in Penang, saying these achievements needed to be communicated more effectively to the public.
"DAP has been in Penang for more than 18 years and in the federal government for about three years.
"There are many things we have done that must be explained to the people," he said.
He highlighted the minimum wage increases, expanded social protection for gig workers, and education policies as among the initiatives, aimed at broadening access regardless of race or background.
Sim reiterated that engagement with the public was not optional for leaders.
"It is compulsory for us not only to listen but also to go down to the ground to explain," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nafas to leverage Malaysia-Australia cooperation to secure fertiliser supply chain
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The National Farmers Organisation (Nafas) will keep working with the government and industry leaders to leverage Malaysia-Australia cooperation, secure the fertiliser supply chain and boost national food security.
In a statement today, Nafas welcomed the Agriculture and Food Security Ministry's announcement following recent talks between Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and his Australian counterpart, Anthony Albanese, to strengthen bilateral cooperation and ensure stable agricultural input supplies.
Nafas said G2G (government-to-government) cooperation is a significant step toward securing critical raw material for the fertiliser industry amid global supply chain uncertainties and the ongoing energy crisis, which directly impacts agriculture and food production.
"As the agency responsible for fertiliser supply to the agricultural sector, Nafas prioritises stable raw materials like CIRP (Christmas Island Rock Phosphate) to ensure continuous and efficient domestic fertiliser production.
"The national annual requirement for CIRP is estimated at 65,000 metric tonnes, mainly for producing compound fertilisers under the subsidised input scheme for nearly 200,000 padi farmers nationwide, depending on seasonal crop needs and agricultural demand," the statement read.
CIRP is a key component in compound fertiliser production, particularly to support the padi sector, the backbone of national rice supply security.
Malaysia also has an edge in nitrogen-based fertiliser production, with its main urea supply sourced from Petronas through strong domestic capacity.
"This complementary approach between Malaysia and Australia covers the entire fertiliser value chain, from raw materials to downstream products," the statement added.
Nafas said supply guarantees from this strategic cooperation will reduce disruption risks, support local fertiliser production stability and ensure continuity of national rice output.
Guaranteed access to these critical raw materials will also sustain domestic fertiliser production, boosting padi farmer productivity and national rice supply stability.
"This cooperation will strengthen the agricultural sector's resilience and keep critical input supplies stable amid growing global challenges," the statement read.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kg Bahagia fire must be 'turning point' for safer housing, says MP
Article:
SANDAKAN: The massive fire at Kampung Bahagia must serve as a turning point toward developing more planned and secure settlements, said Batu Sapi member of parliament Datuk Khairul Firdaus Akbar Khan.
Khairul Firdaus said the government has taken a significant step by gazetting the settlement as village land, paving the way for more systematic development.
He plans to discuss the necessity of building a People's Housing Project (PPR) with the state government to house the fire victims.
"We will ensure that we can assist the victims in rebuilding their homes. Once this village is gazetted, we will discuss with the state government the construction of a PPR," he said after visiting the victims today.
Khairul Firdaus, who is also Deputy Human Resources Minister, said all parties are working hard to ensure aid is channelled effectively to ease the victims' burden.
He added that his team remains on the ground to monitor support efforts.
"Beyond immediate aid, our priority is to ensure that the resettlement of victims can be provided within this village," he said.
It is understood that the process of gazetting the 24-hectare water settlement as village land is currently underway.
The fire, which broke out at 1.30am today, destroyed approximately 1,000 houses and left more than 9,000 residents homeless.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Three feared dead after gunman opens fire at food court
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: At least three people are feared dead after a
gunman opened fire
at a food court in Taman Kota Jaya in a brazen daylight shooting today.
District police chief Superintendent Yusof Othman confirmed the incident but declined to disclose the exact number of victims.
Police are at the scene and investigations are underway, he told the New Straits Times.
The shooting occurred at 1.45pm at the Medan Selera Taman Kota Jaya food court, an eatery known locally as kedai bulat due to its circular layout.
A resident, identified only as Yogi, 36, said a man in his 50s entered the food court and fired several shots.
"At least three people were hit. One is believed to be one of the outlet proprietors," he said.
The suspect is believed to have driven from the scene to a restaurant less than 100 metres away, where he sat down.
"He did not run. He just walked and sat there," Yogi said.
It is understood that police moved in shortly after and arrested the suspect at the second location.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: Biofuel shows potential as new alternative energy source
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Malaysia currently has 19 biofuel production plants based on rubber waste, with a monthly capacity of approximately 1.5 million litres.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said, as such, biofuels have the potential to become a new alternative energy source amid the global energy crisis.
He said the biofuel production initiative had been presented to the National Economic Action Council (NEAC) through collaboration between government agencies and private companies in the plantation sector.
"There are now 19 biofuel production plants ranging from B15 to B50. We will use sludge or by-products from CPO (crude palm oil) production, where about 35 per cent of the sludge will be fully utilised to produce biofuel as well as Jet A1 fuel.
"This has already been initiated, and NEAC has in principle accepted the proposal. We will coordinate with the relevant bodies and companies. We want to ensure that the production cost is low when synergy is achieved among the 19 plants," he said.
Zahid said this to reporters after opening the Rissmart Grocer supermarket here today.
Also present was Rubber Industry Smallholders Development Authority (Risda) chairman Manndzri Nasib.
Zahid, who is also the rural and regional development minister, said the government expects that large-scale production will be able to reduce operating costs, thereby lowering the overall price of the fuel.
He added that the biofuel has also undergone capability testing for use in vehicles and is believed to have the potential to stabilise and reduce diesel prices in the long term.
"We have conducted pilot tests, and Petronas has also tested this diesel. We are also collaborating with several vehicle manufacturers to test the suitability of this biodiesel for all vehicles produced in our country," he said.
Previously, Risda was reported to be in the process of finalising a preliminary study on the development of a biofuel product derived from rubber scrap, known as "green rubber fuel".
This resulted from a pilot project by the Transformation for New Generation (TR4NG) innovation group under the Risda district office in Tanah Merah, Kelantan.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illegal racers ride against traffic to evade police cordons
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Riding against the flow of traffic has been identified as one of the tactics used by illegal street racers or mat rempit to evade police cordons during an integrated operation along Jalan Tun Razak early this morning.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Investigation and Enforcement Department chief assistant commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa said the dangerous manoeuvre was detected throughout Op Mabuk and Op Samseng Jalanan conducted from 10pm yesterday until 7am today, involving 102 policemen.
"In the chaos, some riders were willing to go against traffic in an attempt to escape, but their efforts were unsuccessful," he told a media conference at the operation site.
The integrated operation also involved personnel from the Road Transport Department (RTD), the National Anti-Drug Agency (Nada) and Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL).
He said a total of 750 motorcycles and 80 cars were inspected, with 751 summonses issued for various offences and 98 motorcycles seized, while RTD issued 59 summons notices and Nada detained two people for taking drugs.
Zamzuri said the two people were arrested after being tested positive for methamphetamine, while five others were detained for driving under the influence of alcohol and are undergoing a second breath test.
"The motorcycles seized were for various offences, including illegal modifications and absence of brakes, with one arrest made under Section 108 of the Road Transport Act 1987 for altering registration numbers," he said.
He said enforcement operations would be intensified following numerous public complaints, particularly on illegal street racing activities around Menara Tabung Haji Selborn along Jalan Tun Razak.
Zamzuri also advised parents to monitor their children's movements and reminded road users not to drive under the influence of alcohol.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Revive the Bandung spirit for century of peace
Article:
THE 20th century was the most violent in human history because modern Western culture perfected industrialised slaughter.
Its instinct to resolve conflicts through violence, its greed for wealth and resources, and its habit of dehumanising entire populations produced horrors that still echo today.
Two world wars killed tens of millions. But the signature act of Western violence remains the atomic bomb.
On Aug 6, 1945, the United States dropped a nuclear weapon on Hiroshima, a city of 350,000 civilians.
Three days later, Nagasaki. Over 200,000 men, women and children were incinerated or died from radiation poisoning. The victims were mothers holding infants, schoolchildren, grandparents. Japan was already seeking surrender.
The bombs were a demonstration: the West would annihilate entire non-western civilian populations to secure dominance. That logic was rehearsed in the colonies long before it reached Europe.
Take Belgium in the Congo. Between 1885 and 1908, King Leopold II's regime turned the Congo into a forced labour camp for rubber. Villages that failed quotas had their inhabitants' hands chopped off - men, women, and children. An estimated 10 million, or about half the population, died.
The Dutch followed the same playbook in Indonesia. For decades, the Cultivation System forced starvation rations on locals.
During Indonesia's independence struggle, Dutch "police actions" massacred over 100,000 civilians through scorched-earth tactics. Farmers were bayoneted in their rice paddies, and villages burned to ash.
Nor were the French any different. In Algeria, from 1954 to 1962, French forces tortured systematically, bombed civilian villages, and threw prisoners from hovering helicopters.
Over one million Algerians died. While bodies floated in the Mediterranean, the world watched in silence. Across the Pacific, the United States replicated the same cruelties in the Philippines.
After seizing the islands in 1898, US troops invented the "water cure" - forcing gallons of salt water down prisoners' throats until their stomachs burst.
Concentration zones depopulated entire provinces. In Balangiga, American soldiers massacred hundreds of Filipino men, women, and children. The American occupation caused the deaths of more than two hundred thousand civilians.
These were not aberrations. They were the same logic of domination and resource extraction that later produced the world wars.
Belgian, Dutch, British, French, American - all competed ruthlessly for territory and wealth. And whenever local populations resisted, the response was indiscriminate violence, collective punishment, and deliberate civilian slaughter.
That pattern did not end with decolonisation. It simply moved to new theatres.
Today, in the 21st century, we watch it unfold in Gaza.
Israel's military campaign, armed and funded by Western powers, has killed over 70,000 Palestinians since October 2023 - the vast majority civilians.
Whole families erased from civil registries. Hospitals bombed. Journalists and aid workers killed. Although the UN Commission of Enquiry in 2025 has called it a genocide, Western governments continue to supply weapons and diplomatic cover to Israel.
Palestinian lives are treated as expendable. Worse, the nuclear threat has returned.
During the current Israel-Iran confrontation, senior Israeli officials have openly discussed pre-emptive nuclear strikes against Iran.
[[nid:1421334]]
United States contingency plans reportedly include tactical nuclear options that would irradiate cities and kill hundreds of thousands of civilians.
The same logic that justified Hiroshima is being revived: when a Western-backed power faces a non-Western adversary, the ultimate weapon becomes conceivable.
We stand at a stark historical crossroads. Will the same Western powers - the United States, Israel, Nato - once again dictate the 21st century, sliding us back into world wars, genocides, and atomic annihilation?
There is always another path. It was articulated in April 1955, when 29 newly independent Asian and African nations gathered in Bandung, Indonesia. They represented more than half of humanity.
They rejected colonialism in all its forms. They called for peaceful coexistence, mutual respect, non-interference, and economic cooperation among the Global South.
The Bandung Conference gave birth to the Non-Aligned Movement and a truly universal vision - one not based on Western individualism, competition, or the threat of force.
Bandung drew on deep civilisational foundations that the West abandoned. From Asia: Confucian harmony, Buddhist non-violence, Islamic justice. From Africa: Ubuntu - "I am because we are" - placing community above individual accumulation.
From Indigenous America: seventh-generation thinking, deciding not for quarterly profits but for descendants unborn.
These are not naive ideals. They sustained societies for millennia before Western colonialism shattered them.
The 21st century can still take the Bandung path - toward peace and shared prosperity for all. But that requires rejecting the Western playbook: hyper-individualism, short-term greed, the normalisation of civilian slaughter.
And it requires embracing traditional values: community over competition, reciprocity over extraction, a truly universal vision of human dignity.
This is a call to action for the Global South. The nations of Africa, Asia, Latin America, and the Pacific - who suffered centuries of colonial violence and now watch Gaza burn - do not have to accept the Western script.
Strengthen South–South cooperation through BRICS, the African Union, Asean. Build independent media that tells the truth about colonial violence.
Demand accountability for war crimes without Western vetoes. Revive the spirit of Bandung for the 21st century: a new conference, a new declaration, a new commitment to non-violence.
The 20th century was the West's century of blood and domination. The question before us is whether the 21st century will be the Global South's century of peace.
The victims of Hiroshima, of the Congo, of Algeria, of the Philippines, and now of Gaza deserve nothing less.
* The writer is chairman of Citizens International, a non-governmental organisation based in Penang dedicated to internationalism, peaceful coexistence and global cooperation for a better world for all
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TTDI mud flood: Residents claim warning signs were ignored
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Residents living near Jalan Haji Openg in Taman Tun Dr Ismail (TTDI) claim there were clear warning signs of a possible collapse before
yesterday's mud flood
at a nearby construction site.
Speaking to the New Straits Times at the scene, resident Aniza Anuar, 52, said they had long anticipated such an incident since construction began on the hill.
"Based on the way the retaining wall was shaping up, with construction taking place above it, we expected something like this to happen," she said.
"Fortunately, it did not become more serious and was limited to a mudslide, with some damaged zinc sheets and parts of the retaining wall affected."
Aniza said land clearing for the National Landscape Department (JLN) project resumed in 2023, involving about six acres (2.4ha) of land.
It is understood that the development order for the project was issued in October 2022.
She said prior to that, there were proposals for other projects, but nothing materialised and the land was left untouched until JLN took over.
"This used to be a hill, which is why the land is higher than the residential area below.
"We initially objected to the plan, but after assurances were given by the Public Works Department and Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL), we did not pursue the matter," she said, adding that residents had even held a protest in July last year after experiencing mud floods at least twice.
A contractor by profession, Aniza said she had earlier raised concerns with the residents' association about cracks and slanting observed on the retaining wall — signs she believed pointed to a possible collapse.
"But before anything could be done, this happened," she said.
She added that the mudflood, which occurred about 5pm, was the result of prolonged heavy rain.
The downpour, which began about 3.30pm and lasted until 5.30pm, had already caused mud and debris from the project site to spill onto a road adjoining the residential area, located less than 15m away.
Another resident, Kim Liew, said zinc sheets covering parts of the construction site had come loose and were visibly displaced even before the mudslide.
"There were already signs earlier in the day. The barriers were not holding, and mud had started flowing out," she said.
Liew said she now feared that further rain today could again wash mud, debris and soil into their houses due to the sloping terrain of the project site.
"I've stayed here for over 45 years and have never seen so much mud wash up to our entrance. This land used to be part of the Bukit Kiara Federal Park before JLN took over the site to build its own building.
"At that time, the land was a filled slope with well-rooted trees. When the trees were cleared, the soil weakened, which led to a series of problems, including the risk of a natural disaster," she said when met at Lorong Abang Haji Openg 4.
TTDI Residents Association chairman Abdul Jabar Ahmad Junid said he had raised the mud flood issue with the authorities, particularly DBKL, but it remained unresolved.
Describing yesterday's incident as extraordinary, Jabar said it was the first time in decades he had seen drains overwhelmed.
"This is the first time we have seen the drains overflow to this extent, with muddy water spilling onto the road and soil washing down from the slope," he said.
The mud flood sent water and debris cascading from the slope into the construction site, causing significant flooding within the project area.
Abdul Jabar said the affected plot is part of Bukit Kiara Federal Park, which had been handed over to the government, particularly the National Landscape Department, for the construction of its office.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kampung Bahagia fire: Victims rise to over 9,000
Article:
SANDAKAN: The number of victims in the Kampung Bahagia fire has risen to more than 9,000, after a massive blaze destroyed about 1,000 stilt houses at the water village early today.
District police chief Assistant Commissioner George Abd Rakman said police received a report on the fire at 1.30am, and the area was declared a disaster zone by 4am.
"The area comprises about 1,200 houses, of which approximately 1,000 were affected, involving 9,007 residents.
"Thankfully, the fire was brought under control by the Fire and Rescue Department," he told reporters at the scene.
[[nid:1421338]]
George said authorities are coordinating the registration of victims for evacuation and the distribution of aid.
He also dismissed social media claims that the fire had resulted in fatalities.
"There are claims of deaths. I wish to stress that, so far, I have not received any reports of loss of life. Those injured were likely hurt while trying to save their belongings or assisting others," he said.
Earlier, Sandakan Fire and Rescue Station chief Jimmy Lagung said the blaze engulfed an area of more than four hectares.
He said the low tide hampered efforts to secure an adequate open water source for firefighting.
"Besides seawater, operations utilised water from a tanker and a factory's pressurised hydrant. Four hose lines stretching 1,000 feet and 12 water jets were used to extinguish the flames.
"A Fire Rescue Tender from the Kinabatangan station assisted by deploying two hose lines and four water jets."
Jimmy said the operation concluded at noon, and the cause of the fire is under investigation.
Disaster management efforts are being carried out in coordination with the Civil Defence Force and other relevant agencies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM1.8mil boost for HSNI facilities, RM3.4mil for district clinics – Dzulkefly
Article:
BATU PAHAT: The Health Ministry has approved a RM1.8 million special allocation to upgrade facilities at Sultanah Nora Ismail Hospital (HSNI) to bolster healthcare delivery for residents in the district.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the funding includes an additional RM800,000 to upgrade the Visitor Centre and improve existing infrastructure and assets.
"The funds will also cover the procurement of medical and non-medical equipment, as well as paving works at the hospital parking area.
"The ministry's main focus is to ensure that healthcare facilities at the grassroots level are continuously improved in line with current developments," he said in a post on X today.
The post followed a two-day working visit to Batu Pahat, which concluded yesterday.
Dzulkefly said a further RM3.421 million has been allocated to upgrade four health clinics in the district: Parit Yaani, Seri Gading, Tongkang Pechah, and Parit Raja. He said this was to ensure a more equitable distribution of healthcare resources.
He added that the country's current stability has enabled the government to manage the economy effectively despite challenges such as the global energy crisis, allowing for continued investment in healthcare.
The minister had visited the four health clinics starting Friday before his visit to HSNI yesterday.
Established in 1894, HSNI operates with a capacity of 610 beds and serves more than 500,000 residents, making it one of the busiest hospitals in Johor after Johor Baru.
During the visit, Dzulkefly inspected key facilities, including the proposed site for the Day Treatment Unit, medical wards, and the Emergency and Radiology departments, to assess the hospital's readiness and service quality.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Penang NRD praised for compassionate response to pregnant woman seeking help
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The
Penang National Registration Department
(NRD) has drawn praise for its compassionate and non-judgmental response to a pregnant woman, in a move seen as a proactive effort to prevent baby dumping.
The exchange began on Threads when the woman sought advice on how to safely place her child for adoption after birth.
She said she was seven months pregnant out of wedlock and had no support from the baby's father.
"I am considering placing the child in a safe haven once the baby is born, as I am unmarried and the father refuses to take responsibility," she wrote.
The Penang NRD advised her not to abandon the infant and provided guidance on legal steps to protect the child's welfare.
"Please register the birth of the child first as a single mother so that the child can obtain their rights as a citizen. Do not leave the child in a public place," the department replied.
Home Minister Datuk Seri Saifuddin Nasution Ismail later reshared the exchange on social media in support of the approach.
The NRD also said that if she is unable to raise the child, legal adoption through a statutory declaration would be the safest option after obtaining a birth certificate.
"Give the child a chance at a better life," it added.
Netizens described the response as a model for how government agencies should engage with the public.
"This is the no-judgment approach we need. Instead of shaming, they offered a solution that could save a life," one user commented.
Another wrote: "By being kind and informative, they likely prevented a tragedy. We need more empathy in the civil service."
The response has been widely seen as an example of how empathy and clear guidance can support individuals in distress while safeguarding vulnerable lives.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Stronger prevention, public awareness in high-risk areas to curb corruption - MACC
Article:
ALOR STAR: Prevention and public awareness must be strengthened alongside enforcement in high-risk areas vulnerable to smuggling, cross-border crime, duty-free abuse, and customs issues, said the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC).
Its Deputy Chief Commissioner (Management and Professional) Datuk Junipah Wandi said such issues have implications for national security and the economy.
She said it would not only impair administrative efficiency but also erode investor confidence, tarnish the nation's image, and harm the public's overall well-being.
"The fact is, every loophole in the system creates an opportunity for corruption.
"If these are not plugged, they grow into syndicates, become ingrained as a culture, and eventually threaten the nation.
"To that end, alongside enforcement, we must keep strengthening prevention and public awareness efforts," she said at the opening of the Kedah MACC Langkawi branch office, according to a statement released today.
She said Langkawi is not merely a tourist spot but a key national gateway with intense flows of people, goods, and economic activity, noting that the Langkawi MACC office forms part of the national effort to reinforce enforcement integrity and plug loopholes that threaten national interests.
"Operating from this new location is not just about relocation but a step to enhance the organisation's capacity for more effective and structured enforcement, prevention, and monitoring," she said.
She added that the MACC hopes the Langkawi office will become a more responsive action hub, not just receiving tip-offs but capable of detecting, investigating, and acting swiftly and decisively.
"Society's role as MACC's eyes and ears is no longer a choice. It is a collective duty to rid the country of corruption. We must shut down opportunities for graft before they take root," she said.
The Langkawi MACC branch was established on Dec 1, 1993, in the Tabung Haji building before moving to the Langkawi Development Authority (LADA) complex in 1996.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Who is he to deprive us?' Iran fires back at Trump on nuclear issue
Article:
DUBAI: US President Donald Trump has no justification to deprive Iran of its nuclear rights, the Iranian Student News Agency quoted Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian as saying on Sunday, as Washington and Teheran continue to face disagreements over nuclear issues.
"Trump says Iran cannot make use of its nuclear rights but doesn't say for what crime. Who is he to deprive a nation of its rights?" Pezeshkian was quoted as saying. --REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Breaking the silence on mental health at work
Article:
LETTERS:
We are living in a time when conversations about productivity, performance, and profitability dominate the workplace. Yet, one critical element continues to be overlooked or, worse, deliberately ignored — mental health.
"Breaking the Silence: Mental Health Matters at Work" is not just a theme; it is a call to action.
For far too long, employees have suffered in silence, burdened by stress, anxiety, depression, burnout, and workplace pressures without adequate support or understanding.
The stigma surrounding mental health has created barriers that prevent individuals from seeking help, often leading to devastating consequences for both employees and organisations.
Mental health is not a personal issue alone, it is a workplace issue.
A mentally healthy workforce is more engaged, productive, and resilient. Conversely, poor mental health leads to absenteeism, presenteeism, reduced performance, and increased healthcare costs. More importantly, it affects lives, families, and communities.
Employers must recognise that creating a safe and supportive psychosocial work environment is not optional — it is a responsibility. This includes:
* Promoting open conversations about mental health without fear of stigma or discrimination;
* Providing access to mental health resources, counselling, and employee assistance programmes;
* Training managers to recognise early signs of mental distress and respond with empathy;
* Ensuring reasonable workloads, work-life balance, and fair treatment at all levels;
* Establishing clear policies to address workplace bullying, harassment, and toxic cultures.
At the same time, employees must also be encouraged to prioritise their mental well-being, seek help when needed, and support one another in fostering a culture of care and understanding.
The government and relevant authorities should further strengthen policies and enforcement mechanisms to ensure that mental health in the workplace is safeguarded.
This includes integrating mental health into occupational safety and health frameworks and encouraging organisations, especially small and medium enterprises, to adopt best practices.
Breaking the silence requires courage, but more importantly, it requires commitment. Commitment from employers to care, from employees to speak up, and from society to normalise mental health conversations.
No one should have to suffer in silence. It is time we acknowledge that mental health matters — at every workplace, at every level, and for every individual.
Together, let us break the silence and build workplaces that truly care.
TAN SRI LEE LAM THYE
Mental Health Advisory Council member
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid: More big names expected to return to Umno via Rumah Bangsa
Article:
SUNGAI BULOH: More political figures and leaders, including those previously with the opposition, are expected to return to Umno through the Rumah Bangsa initiative.
Umno president and Barisan Nasional chairman Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said many of these leaders had already met with him.
"I expect more big names to join. They have met me informally. Many have come to see me, but to protect their reputations, I do not need to mention their names here. We welcome all parties to join Umno without conditions," he said.
He said this at a press conference after officiating the RissMart Grocer here today.
Also present were Plantation and Commodities Minister Datuk Seri Dr Noraini Ahmad and Risda chairman Mandzri Nasib.
Zahid said Umno would simplify the process for those wishing to return through the Rumah Bangsa committee, chaired by vice-president Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin.
He cited the examples of Datuk Seri Hishammuddin Hussein, Khairy Jamaluddin, and Tan Sri Syed Hamid Albar, noting their re-entries were facilitated quickly without going through a committee.
"We have simplified the applications; they only need to write a letter expressing their intent to join. We decided these applications do not need to be referred to a committee. We have also empowered the Rumah Bangsa committee to proceed with these matters immediately," he said.
Zahid added that Umno is also streamlining membership for youth through online applications, removing complicated and lengthy approval processes.
"We have opened online applications and will issue digital membership cards. This is also specifically for those who left the party previously.
"In the past, the conventional way required them to fill out forms and apply through branch chiefs, before being sent to the division, state, and headquarters.
"This process was disliked by the youth. They can apply directly online to the headquarters. That is why we are getting a good response," he said.
On Friday, Umno secretary-general Datuk Seri Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said 6,252 former leaders and members at various levels had been accepted back into the party under the Rumah Bangsa initiative.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Zahid praises national Quran recitation and memorisation champs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi congratulated the champions of the National Quran Recitation and Memorisation Competition 1447H/2026M.
In a Facebook post today, Zahid said he hoped their success at the event, which wrapped up in Terengganu last night, would inspire young people to draw closer to the Quran, understand it better and reflect its teachings in their daily lives.
"May both of them be blessed with success as they carry Malaysia's flag on the international stage. Insya-Allah," he said.
The men's title went to Cik Hasnul Taqiyuddin Cik Sazali of Perak, while Sarawakian Nur Syakura Mohamad Musa won the women's title.
Cik Hasnul Taqiyuddin, 25, is a third-year student of Quran and Sunnah at Universiti Sains Islam Malaysia, while Nur Syakura, 31, is a Quran teacher supervisor with the Sarawak Islamic Council.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran war sparks UK-EU reset as Trump ties fray
Article:
LONDON: Britain's government is set to announce legislation next month to move the country closer to the European Union, as the Iran war sours the UK's so-called special relationship with the United States.
President Donald Trump's unpredictability and stream of insults towards America's historic ally is adding impetus to Prime Minister Keir Starmer's bid to deepen ties with the 27-nation bloc, a decade after Britons narrowly voted to leave the EU.
"We have a government that is already eager to move closer towards the EU, and the events in Iran provide an opportunity to speed up that process," Evie Aspinall, director of the British Foreign Policy Group think-tank, told AFP.
Starmer's administration is preparing an EU "reset" bill that will give ministers powers to align UK standards with EU single market rules as they evolve – something called "dynamic alignment."
King Charles III will announce the legislation on May 13 when he reads out Starmer's legislative plans for the coming months, a government official told AFP on condition of anonymity.
Starmer has repeatedly called for a deeper economic and security relationship with Europe since his Labour party won the 2024 general election, ousting the Conservatives, who had implemented the 2016 Brexit referendum.
He has upped those calls in recent days, telling Dutch leader Rob Jetten on Tuesday that "he believed the partnership between the UK and the bloc needed to be fit for the challenges we were facing today."
The EU is Britain's biggest trading partner, while the International Monetary Fund warned this week that the UK will be the advanced economy hardest hit by the Iran conflict.
"Certainly Iran has made it (the reset) more prescient," said the UK official.
"We need to build economic resilience across the continent," they added.
Starmer refused to involve Britain in the US and Israel's initial strikes on Feb 28, angering Trump, although he has since allowed American forces to use UK bases for a "limited defensive purpose."
Under pressure at home for his disastrous decision to appoint former Jeffrey Epstein associate Peter Mandelson as ambassador to Washington, Starmer has received plaudits for standing up to Trump in the face of repeated taunts from the US president.
Days ago, Trump threatened in a phone interview with Sky News to scrap a US-UK trade deal that limited the impact on Britain of his tariffs blitz.
"There's no doubt that there is now momentum in the UK-EU relationship partly as a result of Trump's unreliable behaviour," David Henig, an expert on UK's post-Brexit trade policy, told AFP.
"Independent UK trade policy looks much harder, the prospects of working with the EU much brighter."
- BREXIT REGRET -
Starmer's administration hopes to table the EU legislation in the next few months, meaning it could come around the time of the 10th anniversary of the Brexit referendum, held in June 2016.
MPs will get to approve whether to provide the government with a mechanism to adopt EU rules – sometimes without a full parliamentary vote – in areas where it has already signed deals with the bloc.
They include a trade agreement designed to ease red tape on food and plant exports and plans for an electricity deal that would integrate the UK into the EU's internal electricity market.
Britain and the EU are also aiming to finalise negotiations on a youth mobility scheme in time for a joint summit in Brussels expected in late June or early July.
Starmer has ruled out rejoining the single market or returning to free movement.
The Liberal Democrats, Britain's traditional third party, wants him to cross one of his other red lines by negotiating a customs union with the EU.
"We need to be doubling down on relations with reliable partners who share our interests and values," the Liberal Democrats foreign affairs spokesman Calum Miller told AFP.
But Brexit remains a toxic issue and the hard-right Reform UK party, leading opinion polls and headed by Eurosceptic firebrand Nigel Farage, have branded the legislation "a betrayal" of the referendum's narrow result.
Surveys regularly now show, however, that most Britons regret the vote to leave the EU, something Starmer hopes to capitalise on.
Rising cost-of-living pressures on family households, which UK finance minister Rachel Reeves has blamed on Trump for starting the war "without a clear exit plan", could also influence minds.
"When the relationship with the United States is fracturing, it means there's reduced opposition to a closer relationship with the EU among the public," said Aspinall.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian soldier charged with war crimes vows to clear his name
Article:
SYDNEY: An Australian former soldier charged with committing war crimes in Afghanistan vowed on Sunday to clear his name, saying he had never "run from a fight" in his first public comments since his arrest.
"For the past 10 years, my family and I have been subject to a campaign to convince Australians that I've acted improperly in my service in Afghanistan," Ben Roberts-Smith told journalists at the Gold Coast.
"I categorically deny all of these allegations, and while I would have preferred these charges not be brought, I will be taking this opportunity to finally clear my name," he said.
Roberts-Smith was granted bail on Friday after a high-profile arrest on five counts of "war crime – murder." Police alleged he was complicit in a string of unlawful killings between 2009 and 2012.
The Victoria Cross recipient has denied all the charges.
He was released from prison on Friday evening after 10 days behind bars.
"I'm proud of my service in Afghanistan. While I was there, I always acted within my values," he said on Sunday.
Australia's most decorated living soldier met Queen Elizabeth II, had his portrait hung at the Australian War Memorial and was honoured as the nation's "father of the year."
But the war hero's reputation was called into question in 2018, when a series of news reports linked him to the alleged murder of unarmed Afghan prisoners by Australian troops.
Roberts-Smith allegedly kicked an unarmed Afghan civilian off a cliff and ordered subordinates to shoot him, The Age and The Sydney Morning Herald reported.
He was also said to have taken part in the machine-gunning of a man who had a prosthetic limb, which he later used as a drinking vessel with other soldiers.
Roberts-Smith has staunchly maintained his innocence throughout, launching legal action against the newspapers that carried the allegations.
But his efforts to sue The Age and The Sydney Morning Herald for defamation backfired, with a judge finding in 2023 that many of the journalists' claims were "substantially true."
Such civil trials carry a lower burden of proof than the criminal proceedings Roberts-Smith now faces.
Australia deployed 39,000 troops to Afghanistan over two decades as part of US- and Nato-led operations against the Taliban and other groups.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trader cheated of RM25,270 in QR code Harumanis scam
Article:
KUALA TERENGGANU: A trader suffered losses amounting to RM25,280 after being duped by a syndicate in an online Harumanis mango sale scam using a quick response (QR) code.
District police chief assistant commissioner Azli Mohd Noor said the 53-year-old victim lodged a report at 7.41pm yesterday after realising unauthorised withdrawals had been made from his bank account.
He said the incident began at about 4pm yesterday when the victim, who was in Marang came across an advertisement for Harumanis mangoes on the social media platform Facebook before contacting the purported seller.
"The suspect later sent a link via the messaging application WhatsApp, which contained a QR code for payment purposes.
"After scanning the code, the victim's mobile phone became unresponsive for about 20 minutes," he said in a statement.
He said the victim subsequently contacted the bank and was informed of three unauthorised transactions amounting to RM8,264, RM8,642 and RM8,374, bringing the total loss to RM25,280.
He said the case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating.
– Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: At Cuba's Bay of Pigs, a victory against US invaders stirs pride
Article:
ORESTES Chamizo points at a scrubby patch of sand on Cuba's Bay of Pigs, showing the spot where a brigade of US-trained Cuban exiles landed on the island's southern coast on April 17, 1961.
"The mercenaries came in right there," Chamizo says, recalling their dramatic defeat and his own role tracking down survivors scattered in the nearby swamp.
He said the US under President Donald Trump, who has repeatedly threatened Cuba since imposing a de facto oil blockade on the island, risks the same outcome if he chooses to invade.
"I'm 90 years old but if I have to pick up a gun again, I'll do it without fear," the still spry Chamizo told Reuters. "The last invasion failed ... and the next one will too."
The prospect of communist-run Cuba resisting a military invasion by the global superpower to the north appears as improbable today as it did 65 years ago.
Reuters spoke with upwards of two dozen residents, young and old, around the Bay of Pigs, long a bastion of national pride after Fidel Castro's victory against the exiles - one of the Cuban Revolution's proudest moments.
The interviews underscore a gulf between the fiery rhetoric of those who lived those early years of the revolution and the island's downtrodden youth who struggle to survive in a Cuba brought to its knees by an inefficient state-run economy and US sanctions.
"Young people here don't have the same spirit they used to," says Miguel Piloto Garcia, a 22-year-old barber who spoke with Reuters from his porch just a few miles from the site of the 1961 US invasion.
"We want to improve our lives, but right now there's no future for us."
[[nid:1421280]]
Cuba and the United States, separated by just 90 miles across the Straits of Florida, are talking, both sides have said.
But Trump has also repeatedly hinted at the possibility of military action against Cuba, telling reporters as recently as March he might be "taking" the island.
"I ​mean, whether I free it, take it. Think I can do anything I want with it," Trump said.
Cuban President Miguel Diaz-Canel last week told NBC that Cuba does not want war.
But he warned Cuba would nonetheless defend itself, if attacked, using "irregular warfare" that would make any incursion by the United States "untenable."
Cuba's constitution requires the country's youth to undergo military training. Recruitment begins at 18 years old.
The service, typically one to two years, includes firearm training, part of a broader "War of All the People" doctrine enshrined in Cuba's National Defence Law and touted by Diaz-Canel in last week's interview.
"Every Cuban man or woman has a mission, a purpose, a place to defend, and they will have their own place to take in the defence," Diaz-Canel said. "So this is all based on people's participation, voluntary participation."
Cuba has held nationwide military drills every Friday - called "National Defense Days" - since the United States in early January invaded Venezuela and deposed former leader Nicolas Maduro.
The training, often aired on Cuba's daily television news programs, shows men and women in urban and rural environments, taking aim with rifles, driving Soviet Union-era tanks, flying drones and tossing grenades.
Despite its increasing visibility, Cuba's military remains a black box to much of the outside world, with no public accounting of its readiness, financing, weaponry or capacity.
[[nid:1421279]]
In a country with no public opinion polls, it remains difficult to gauge support for the country's armed forces.
The rhetoric from both Washington and Havana takes time to reach the Bay of Pigs, a distant backwater surrounded by the Zapata Swamp that now suffers 22 hours of blackouts a day.
Residents have learned to live largely without public transport or modern communications like cell phones and internet.
Yudel Ramos, 30, a young fisherman and crab hunter, is too busy making ends meet to worry much about war.
He says his salary is not even enough to buy him a sack of charcoal, so his time is occupied scavenging for firewood.
"If the time came to give my life for Cuba, I would, but sometimes I don't know what to think," he told Reuters outside his home in Palpite. "We are going through a very difficult time."
Immigration has also sapped the ranks of potential military recruits.
Between 2020 and 2024, the country registered a population decline of more than 1.4 million people, more than 10 per cent of its population, due largely to migration in which young people between 18 and 30 years old predominate, according to recent data from Cuba's ONEI statistics agency.
The changing face of Cuba saddens Jesus Bernardino Alonso, 87, one of the few remaining Bay of Pigs veterans still living in Palpite, a town whose entrance is adorned by a sign marking the defeat of the US-trained invaders.
He recalls how the entire town rallied in response to the invasion 65 years ago.
"It's true times have changed, and young people today ... some don't share the same ideals," Alonso said.
"But there are still many of us who defend this, even though we know we are facing a superpower."
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police raid three condo-based call centres in Sungai Nibong, arrest 11
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police raided three condominiums in Sungai Nibong that were being used as call centres for an online fraud syndicate, arresting 11 people, including foreigners.
Penang police chief Datuk Azizee Ismail said the raids were conducted last Friday by the state's Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID) following intelligence and surveillance.
"The arrests comprise three Chinese men, two Taiwanese men, two Vietnamese men and one woman, one Japanese man, and two local men, aged between 21 and 56.
"Initial investigations found that all suspects were working as customer service agents, operating fake Facebook accounts for online fraud," he said in a statement today.
He said the syndicate is believed to have begun operating in early April this year, targeting victims from China, Japan, Taiwan and Vietnam through those social media platforms.
Among the items seized during the first raid were 11 iPhones, a WiFi router, a set of house keys, an iPad, a laptop, two computer monitors, two CPUs, two keyboards and a TP-Link internet switch.
In the second raid, he said, multiple mobile phones of various brands and a set of house keys were confiscated.
He said during interrogations, the suspects admitted to starting work as customer service agents in early April around Sungai Nibong to carry out the fraudulent activities.
They have been remanded to assist with investigations under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating.
—Bernama
TAGS: Sungai Nibong, condominiums, call centres, online fraud, raids, arrests, Facebook, Aziee Ismail
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muhyiddin says emergency call shows fear of losing polls
Article:
GOMBAK: Calls to declare an emergency to postpone the Melaka and Johor state elections are a sign that the government is afraid of facing defeat, says Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin.
The Bersatu president said such calls were made without solid grounds, suggesting the government was instead worried about unfavourable public perception.
Muhyiddin, who is also Perikatan Nasional (PN) deputy chairman, described the move as an abuse of power, alleging that government machinery was being used to declare an emergency for political purposes.
"There is no reason to declare an emergency. A DAP representative has requested it to postpone the Melaka and Johor elections.
"During the Covid-19 pandemic, an emergency was declared to postpone elections due to health concerns. However, I do not know the reason for seeking a postponement now," he said.
The former prime minister suggested that the push for an emergency stemmed from a fear of losing, as the public remained angry over fuel issues and the cost of living.
He said this at a press conference during the Bersatu Gombak Aidilfitri gathering here yesterday.
Muhyiddin was responding to a proposal by Kota Melaka member of parliament and state DAP chairman Khoo Poay Tiong for the Melaka government to petition the Yang di-Pertuan Agong to declare an emergency.
Khoo had suggested the postponement as the people were facing rising cost-of-living pressures following a fuel crisis.
Muhyiddin, who is Pagoh member of parliament, said PN was ready to face the Melaka and Johor elections whenever they were held.
"We have started preparations at both state and federal levels. We have appointed election directors and other key roles within PN. I see no need to declare an emergency to postpone the elections.
"This is a sign that they are afraid and are misusing the emergency for political ends. I believe this is inappropriate," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DAP must press on with long-term 'Malaysian Dream', says Lim Kit Siang
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: DAP stalwart Tan Sri Lim Kit Siang has called on the party to remain committed to a long-term vision of a "Malaysian Dream" anchored on unity, reform and inclusivity, as it celebrates its 60th anniversary.
Speaking at the party's congress, Lim reflected on DAP's evolution from a small political outfit in the 1960s to a major national party with a broad organisational base.
"I never thought I would live to attend the 60th anniversary celebration," he said, noting the party's six-decade journey.
Lim said DAP had grown from just seven branches in 1966 to about 2,200 branches today, with membership expanding to an estimated 200,000.
While acknowledging the party's expansion and its current position in government, Lim stressed that its founding ideals must remain unchanged.
"Now we have 200,000 members. But our ideals, our principles must continue. We cannot go backwards, only forward and forward," he added.
Lim reiterated that the core aspiration of DAP was to build a fairer, more equal and more united Malaysia, which he described as the "Malaysian Dream".
"We want a more just, more reasonable and more united country. The Malaysian Dream that I thought we joined DAP for — more liberty, more equality, more fairness, a better society," he said.
He also acknowledged Malaysia's multiracial composition, saying any national vision must be built on consensus across all communities, including Malays, Chinese, Indians and others.
"If Chinese have this dream, if Indians have this dream, if others have this dream, but Malays do not, then we cannot achieve it," he said, adding that persuasion and long-term engagement were essential.
He also emphasised that political reform could not be achieved overnight, urging patience and strategic thinking in pursuing change.
"We have long-term thinking. The Malaysian Dream cannot be achieved overnight.
"We must persuade all races to believe in this dream," he said.
He added that DAP's role remained focused on advancing reforms within Malaysia's political framework while maintaining a long-term outlook.
"This is the task of DAP — a long-term objective. We can accept constraints, but we must have long-term vision to realise the Malaysian Dream," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Iran pulls out of DSA 2026 over logistics issues
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Iran has withdrawn from the Defence Services Asia (DSA) and National Security (Natsec) Asia 2026 exhibitions at the eleventh hour due to logistical challenges stemming from the conflict in the Middle East.
Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin said the withdrawal was not politically motivated but resulted from operational difficulties involving the movement and delivery of assets.
"Iran initially expressed readiness to participate, but their companies withdrew due to the challenges they are facing.
"If they want to move or ship products, there are certainly difficulties, including issues related to the Strait of Hormuz and other factors affecting their logistics," he said.
He said this at a press conference after inspecting final preparations for the exhibition at the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (Mitec) here today.
Khaled emphasised that DSA remains a neutral platform and does not include international conflicts as part of its official discussion agenda.
"We want all nations to see peace achieved and conflicts ended as soon as possible. We do not make these issues official subjects in the forum as it could jeopardise ongoing negotiations between the parties involved," he said.
However, he noted that such conflicts are still discussed informally during bilateral meetings between defence leaders.
"In bilateral or closed-door meetings between ministers, deputy ministers and military leadership, these matters are unavoidable.
"Beyond the desire for peace, we also evaluate how current conflicts are conducted and to what extent existing defence approaches remain relevant in the era of new technology," he said.
The DSA and Natsec Asia 2026 exhibition will run for four days starting tomorrow until Thursday. Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim is scheduled to officiate the opening tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Leave PKR if you no longer align with its struggle, Amirudin tells disgruntled members
Article:
GOMBAK: PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said any member or leader who no longer agrees with the party's direction is free to leave.
He said this when asked about Pandan member of parliament Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli, who is alleged to have gone against party decisions on several key issues.
"It is not about any individual. If anyone feels they are no longer compatible with the party's direction, they are welcome to leave," he told reporters after the Gombak PKR annual general meeting today.
Amirudin, who is also Gombak PKR division chief, said his remarks were intended to ensure all leaders and members remained focused on the party's mandate without disrupting internal stability.
"Priority must be given to the party's larger agenda rather than personal polemics that could tarnish the organisation's image in the eyes of the people," he said.
Rafizi, a former PKR deputy president, has recently been criticised for being overly vocal on public platforms, particularly social media.
Some quarters view his open criticism of the government leadership as a violation of party ethics and the principle of musyawarah (consultation).
Reflecting on the party's history, Amirudin said previous betrayals by top leaders failed to cripple PKR because of its loyal grassroots.
"History has proven that individuals can come and go, but the organisation will stand firm if the principles of the struggle are not sacrificed," he said.
He said he believed PKR would continue to grow due to the strength of its members and the cohesion of the team led by Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and Datuk Seri Dr Wan Azizah Wan Ismail.
Amirudin reminded those with party responsibilities to carry out their trust with dedication without prioritising personal agendas.
"Any dissatisfaction should be channelled through the correct internal processes according to the party constitution, rather than through open attacks that only benefit opponents.
"Party discipline is an element that cannot be compromised to ensure all political work proceeds smoothly and effectively," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Nga: DAP's track record key to winning next general election
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: DAP's proven track record in delivering on its promises will be a key factor as the country heads towards the next general election, said its national deputy chairman Nga Kor Ming.
He said the party's credibility, built over decades of struggle and strengthened through tangible results in government, would be central to winning voters' trust.
"When people go to the ballot box, they will judge us based on what we have delivered.
"We are a party that delivers as promised — and that is how we earn trust and confidence," he said at the party's 60th anniversary celebration in Penang.
Nga said DAP's journey from having minimal representation in the 1990s to being part of the federal government today demonstrates its resilience and consistency.
He recalled a time when party events drew only a handful of supporters, compared to the strong backing it enjoys today.
"From five people attending ceramah to thousands standing with us today — that shows who we are.
"We never give up, and we continue to fight for all Malaysians," he said.
Highlighting the party's achievements in government, Nga said DAP had fulfilled key reform promises, including strengthening Parliament through the Parliamentary Services Act and introducing Prime Minister's Question Time.
He added that structural reforms, such as the proposed separation of the roles of Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor and limiting the prime minister's tenure, had also been approved at Cabinet level.
"These are reforms we promised, and today we are delivering them," he said.
Nga also pointed to achievements under ministries led by DAP, particularly in housing and local government.
He said Malaysia recorded RM240 billion in property transactions in 2025, the highest in a decade, signalling a strong recovery in the sector.
In addition, he said the government had surpassed its target of building 500,000 affordable homes, while also reviving more than 1,500 abandoned housing projects, benefiting about 176,000 homebuyers.
On local government, Nga said more than 15,000 public infrastructure projects, including markets, drainage systems and recreational facilities, had been completed nationwide over the past three years.
"These are not just promises. These are real results that impact people's lives," he said.
Nga added that DAP's governance approach emphasises inclusivity, noting increased allocations for non-Muslim houses of worship and dedicated funding for Indian communities, alongside continued support for Chinese new villages.
He stressed that such policies reflect the party's commitment to a multiracial Malaysia where no community is left behind.
Looking ahead, Nga said the party must remain grounded and continue engaging with the rakyat to maintain support.
"There is no shortcut to success. We must stay humble, stay hungry and stay focused, always be on the ground, listen to the people and solve their problems," he said.
With the next general election approaching, Nga said unity within the party and consistent delivery on promises would be crucial.
"The struggle continues. If we remain united and continue to deliver, we will prevail in the coming election," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DAP must remain rakyat's voice in government or risk losing relevance
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: DAP must continue to act as the voice of the people in government or risk losing its relevance and public trust, DAP national chairman Gobind Singh Deo said.
He said the party's role in the federal administration goes beyond holding office, stressing that it must consistently raise rakyat concerns and push for reforms at the highest level.
"The voice of the people must reach the Cabinet. If we are in government and we do not speak up for the rakyat, then our role has no meaning," he said at the party's 60th anniversary celebration in Penang.
He said DAP has been actively advocating institutional reforms within the unity government, including the review of laws such as the Security Offences (Special Measures) Act (Sosma), strengthening the independence of key institutions, and proposals to separate the roles of the Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor.
He said reforms involving enforcement agencies, including the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC), remain a priority in line with the party's long-standing commitment to accountability and good governance.
"These are positions we took even when we were in the opposition. Now that we are in government, we must ensure they are implemented.
"We must honour the promises we made to the people," he added.
Acknowledging the constraints of operating within a unity government, Gobind said consensus-building with coalition partners may slow progress, but insisted this should not dilute the party's resolve.
"There is a lot more that needs to be done. Where we can act, we will act. Where there is resistance, we must find a way forward," he said.
He said delivering on reform promises is crucial to maintaining public confidence and securing continued support in future elections.
Reflecting on the party's journey, Gobind said DAP's rise from the opposition benches to positions of power in Penang and Putrajaya was built on decades of sacrifice by its leaders and members.
He noted that many had faced legal action and detention in the past, shaping the party's core principles and strengthening its resolve.
"These struggles define who we are. The people supported us because of our principles, and we must never abandon them," he said.
He also cautioned against losing sight of the party's foundations amid rapidly changing political and social landscapes, particularly with the influence of social media.
He said it is crucial for younger leaders to understand the party's history and uphold its core values while adapting to new challenges.
Looking ahead, he called for unity across all levels of the party, stressing that collective strength is key to navigating future challenges and elections.
"We must move forward as one team, build each other up, and remain united. Only then can we succeed and continue to serve the people," he said.
Gobind also highlighted the need for political stability and effective governance in the face of global economic uncertainties, including potential spillover effects from geopolitical tensions.
He said DAP must balance its push for institutional reform with efforts to ensure economic stability for Malaysians.
"As we move forward, we must deliver on reforms while ensuring stability and confidence for the rakyat," he said.
The event was part of DAP's nationwide 60th anniversary celebrations, marking its founding in 1966 and reaffirming its commitment to remain a strong and consistent voice for Malaysians.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Melaka Straits secure, not comparable to Strait of Hormuz, says Khaled Nordin
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin has assured that security and stability in the Straits of Melaka remain intact, dismissing concerns that it could face risks similar to the Strait of Hormuz.
He said the situation in the Strait of Hormuz should not be used as a comparison or seen as a precedent for the region.
"Malaysia, Indonesia and Singapore are countries that adhere to international law and uphold the principles of freedom of navigation and trade. It is not appropriate to compare these two routes," he said.
He said this at a press conference after inspecting final preparations for the Defence Security Asia (DSA) 2026 exhibition at the Malaysia International Trade and Exhibition Centre (Mitec) here today.
On a related matter, Khaled said current global conflicts have provided lessons on the changing landscape of modern warfare.
"We are seeing many new technologies. To face challenges from nations with sophisticated assets, the capability to produce assets quickly and at a lower cost is also important.
"We do not necessarily fight aircraft with aircraft. Instead, the use of drones, rockets and missiles in large numbers is seen as a more effective approach," he said.
He also clarified that the participation of companies from various countries in the DSA exhibition did not contradict Malaysia's stance, despite concerns raised by some quarters regarding global conflicts.
"This is a trade exhibition and a neutral platform. We respect the views of all parties, but this event provides us with the opportunity to evaluate and make decisions based on national requirements.
"Ultimately, we may become buyers, and this platform allows us to compare and evaluate available products," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian delegation to carry protest voice on Palestine issue in Brussels
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR April 19 —  Malaysia will send a cross-party delegation of Members of Parliament to an international conference in Brussels to lodge a formal protest against the Israeli Parliament’s move to impose the death penalty by hanging on Palestinian detainees.
PKR vice-president Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the party has named two representatives, Ledang MP Syed Ibrahim Syed Noh and Balik Pulau MP Datuk Muhammad Bakhtiar Wan Chik, to attend the conference.
“We are also awaiting names from other parties. Overall, we expect between six and eight MPs to take part in the convention in Brussels,” he said after delivering a speech at the Gombak PKR division 2026 annual general meeting today.
Amirudin said participation by representatives from various parties, including UMNO and PAS, reflects Malaysia’s firm unity on global humanitarian issues, following the Israeli Parliament’s decision to impose the death penalty without the right of appeal on Palestinians convicted of deadly attacks.
“I believe these representatives will carry the voice of Malaysians who are united in opposing genocide and any form of injustice against our friends in Palestine,” said Amirudin, who is also the Selangor Menteri Besar.
On the Global Sumud Flotilla (GSF) 2.0 mission, he said the humanitarian aid mission is now approaching Sicily, Italy, after facing delays due to weather conditions.
Amirudin explained that he had to return to Malaysia temporarily to attend the State Legislative Assembly sitting after the mission team was stranded for two days in Barcelona due to storm forecasts.
“They had to stop to wait for safe wind conditions along the route. Insya-Allah, I will return there to rejoin the mission after the assembly sitting concludes,” he said.
According to Amirudin, the mission will pass through several key checkpoints, including Greece, Istanbul and Cyprus.
“So I want to see the route I can follow and also ensure that all participants are in good condition, healthy and safe,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Search launched for five-year-old girl feared snatched by crocodile in Lahad Datu
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 19 — A five-year-old girl is missing and feared to have been snatched by a crocodile while playing at a beach near Jalan Felda Sahabat 16, Desa Kencana in Lahad Datu this morning.
According to
Buletin TV3
, Lahad Datu Fire and Rescue Station (BBP) acting chief Mohd Hamka Mustamin said the department received an emergency call at 10.27am via the MERS999 line.
Initial information indicated the incident occurred around 10am, when the child was at the shoreline.
“The victim is believed to have been seized by a crocodile and dragged into the sea. Her full identity has yet to be confirmed.
“A team from the Lahad Datu BBP has been deployed to the scene to carry out search and rescue (SAR) operations,” he said when contacted.
He added that the operation is ongoing with police assistance as efforts continue to locate the child.
Firefighters are facing challenges due to the distance to the location — about 101km from the station — as well as hazardous conditions at the site.

Title: Three killed in broad daylight shooting in Kota Tinggi, suspect caught trying to flee
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 19 — Three people were killed in a shooting outside a food outlet in Kota Jaya, Kota Tinggi in Johor Bahru, this afternoon.
According to
Kosmo! Online
, the incident, which occurred at about 1.40pm, left two men and a woman dead.
Their identities have yet to be established.
Police detained a man suspected of involvement shortly after the shooting while he was attempting to flee.
Johor police chief Datuk Ab Rahaman Arsad confirmed the incident when contacted, adding that officers had seized a pistol believed to have been used in the attack.
“Investigations are ongoing. Police will issue a statement in the near future,” he said.

Title: Zambry: UM report admits ‘sensitive’ elements in theatre row, corrective steps underway
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 19 — Universiti Malaya (UM) has acknowledged “sensitive” elements in a recent theatre performance that drew online backlash, submitting a report to the Higher Education Ministry.
According to
The Star
, Higher Education Minister Datuk Seri Zambry Abdul Kadir said the university, in its report, admitted that parts of the performance “were sensitive and required improvement”, adding that steps were already under way to address the issue.
Speaking to reporters after a walkabout at the Sabah Zone II Jom Masuk Universiti 2026 programme at Universiti Malaysia Sabah in Kota Kinabalu, Zambry said creative expression must still sit within accepted norms.
“However, sometimes when creative programmes are carried out by students, they are viewed from different perspectives,” he said, noting public concern and criticism over the performance.
He stressed that while universities have their own guidelines governing student productions, corrective measures were necessary to avoid offending the sensitivities of Malaysia’s multiracial and multireligious society.
Zambry also urged arts and creative faculties nationwide to exercise greater caution in handling sensitive themes, but pushed back against calls for harsher action.
Asked whether shutting down the faculty involved was an option, he said such a move would be excessive.
Closing a faculty, he added, would be “more reactive than proactive” and risk triggering further problems, arguing instead for improvements within existing frameworks.
The controversy stems from a performance staged under the Carnival Theatre (Karviter) competition earlier this week, where a brief scene circulated online and was criticised by some as containing suggestive elements.
UM has since apologised, saying the performance breached competition rules after deviating from the approved script submitted beforehand.

Title: Zahid: New ‘green rubber fuel’ could ease rising energy costs
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 19 — Malaysia currently has 19 biofuel production plants based on rubber waste, with a monthly capacity of approximately 1.5 million litres, thus having the potential to become a new alternative energy source amid the global energy crisis.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said that the biofuel production initiative had been presented to the National Economic Action Council (MTEN) through collaboration between government agencies and private companies in the plantation sector.
“There are now 19 biofuel production plants ranging from B15 to B50. We will use sludge or by-products from CPO production, where about 35 per cent of the sludge will be fully utilised to produce biofuel as well as Jet A1 fuel.
“This has already been initiated, and MTEN has in principle accepted the proposal. We will coordinate with the relevant bodies and companies. We want to ensure that the production cost is low when synergy is achieved among the 19 plants,” he said.
He said this to the media after officiating Rissmart Grocer supermarket here today. Also present was the Rubber Industry Smallholders Development Authority (Risda) chairman Manndzri Nasib.
